learningOutcomes: Describe the main idea of the topic.
Apply the lecture ideas in a simple example.

slides.title: Short theory
slides.text:
Temperature Measurement

Background

Most temperature measurement methods can be classified into two main categories: contact and non-contact.

In contact methods, heat exchange between the sensor and the measured object occurs through conduction and convection. In non-contact methods (also known as pyrometric methods), temperature is determined by measuring thermal radiation.

This exercise focuses on contact temperature measurement using electrical sensors. In modern industrial and electronic systems, temperature is measured primarily using electrical thermometers with thermosensitive primary transducers.

These transducers can be classified as active or passive. Passive transducers are widely used and rely on the dependence of electrical resistance on temperature.

Two main types of passive thermosensitive elements are used:

  • Metallic resistance temperature detectors (RTDs), where resistance increases with temperature (positive temperature coefficient).
  • Thermistors, typically semiconductor devices with a negative temperature coefficient (NTC), characterized by much higher sensitivity but strong nonlinearity.

RTDs provide high accuracy and stability but relatively low sensitivity, while thermistors offer high sensitivity at the cost of nonlinear behavior, which must be compensated in practical applications.

Resistance–temperature dependence (click to enlarge)

A simple low-power thermostat is shown in the figure below. It uses a thermistor sensor in a bridge circuit. The bridge output is amplified by an operational amplifier, which drives a heater transistor supplying current to the heater resistors. These resistors may be omitted if the transistor can safely dissipate the required power and is provided with an adequate heat sink.

The temperature is adjusted by varying the resistance of one bridge arm using a preset resistor.

The device can be used for temperature control in small enclosed environments, such as compact ovens or chambers requiring stable thermal conditions.

Thermostat circuit (click to enlarge)

slides.title: Where a stable temperature can be an advantage
slides.text:
An example application of the apparatus described in the previous section is maintaining a crystal oscillator at a nearly constant temperature. Such components are critical in many precision instruments.

The accuracy of digital timers and frequency counters depends primarily on the stability and precision of their internal time reference. In many cases, this reference is provided by a quartz oscillator.

The accuracy of such oscillators is determined by the properties of the quartz resonator. Quartz is a natural piezoelectric material.

Different quartz cut orientations relative to crystallographic axes (click to enlarge)

When a quartz crystal is cut at a specific orientation relative to its crystallographic axes, it exhibits strong piezoelectric behavior. Under an applied alternating electric field, the crystal undergoes mechanical deformation (inverse piezoelectric effect), while mechanical vibrations generate an electric response (direct piezoelectric effect).

Quartz Crystal (click to enlarge)

As a result, an electromechanical oscillatory system can be established, where standing waves are formed within the crystal. This mechanism allows the crystal to act as a highly stable, frequency-selective element.

Similar to a mechanical oscillator, where energy alternates between kinetic and potential forms, in a crystal oscillator the energy oscillates between mechanical and electrical domains. A key advantage of the crystal oscillator is its extremely sharp resonance, resulting in high frequency stability and selectivity.

Mechanical oscillator (click to enlarge)

The resonant frequency is primarily determined by the physical dimensions of the crystal, being inversely proportional to its thickness. Quartz resonators are characterized by a very high quality factor (Q > 2·10⁴) and excellent frequency stability (δf/f ≈ 10⁻⁵ to 10⁻⁶).

A key limitation is the dependence of the resonant frequency on the ambient temperature, which must be considered in precision applications.

Relative Frequency Stability (click to enlarge)

In this role, the temperature regulator shown above can be used as an approximate temperature stabilizer.

Just like in an hourglass, where the interval between the “ticks” is determined by the physical constraints of the system - the size and shape of the sand grains, the geometry of the glass, and so on - the “ticks” of an atomic clock are set by its internal constraints.

In this case, these constraints are the laws of quantum mechanics, which are universal and do not change with time or location.

In principle, the atomic clock is not much different than a simple hourglass watch (click to enlarge)

In principle, an atomic clock follows the same idea as a simple hourglass: the beat interval is determined by a highly stable and repeatable physical process.

In an atomic clock, the natural oscillation frequency of an atom is used as the time reference.

Time is measured by counting the total number of clock ticks using a simple counter.

Time measurement principle (click to enlarge)

slides.title: Using a diode as a sensor
slides.text:
The voltage drop across a semiconductor junction decreases approximately linearly with temperature, with a typical rate of about 2 to 2.4 mV/°C. For silicon-based devices, this relationship remains sufficiently linear over a wide temperature range, typically from about −50°C to +150°C.

An important requirement is that the forward current producing the voltage drop across the diode (used here as a primary transducer) must be maintained within a controlled range, typically around 10 µA.
